seoTitle: ECEC Crystal Resonators & Oscillators | Authorized Distributor | BeiLuo
seoDescription: Authorized distributor of ECEC quartz crystals, crystal oscillators, and timing components. Technical support, competitive pricing, fast delivery.

faqs.question: How do I select the right ECEC timing component for my application?
faqs.answer: When selecting ECEC timing components, consider: 1) Component type - crystal resonators for cost-sensitive applications with external oscillator circuits, crystal oscillators for guaranteed startup and simplified design. 2) Frequency requirements - match your system clock exactly, common frequencies include 32.768kHz, 8MHz, 12MHz, 16MHz, 24MHz, 48MHz. 3) Package size - HC-49/S for through-hole, SMD 2.0x1.6mm to 7.0x5.0mm for surface mount. 4) Frequency tolerance - ±10ppm to ±50ppm depending on accuracy needs. 5) Operating temperature - commercial (0 to +70C) or industrial (-40 to +85C). 6) Special features - TCXO for temperature stability, VCXO for voltage control. Contact our FAE team for selection assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our ECEC timing component categories or contact our technical team for application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between HC-49/S and SMD crystal packages?
faqs.answer: HC-49/S is a through-hole cylindrical metal package, typically 11.0x4.7mm in size. It offers excellent mechanical stability and is suitable for applications requiring through-hole mounting. SMD (Surface Mount Device) packages are compact rectangular ceramic packages ranging from 2.0x1.6mm to 7.0x5.0mm. SMD packages are ideal for automated assembly and space-constrained applications. Key differences: HC-49/S - Larger size, through-hole mounting, excellent stability, lower cost. SMD - Compact size, automated assembly compatible, various sizes available, slightly higher cost. Choose HC-49/S for cost-sensitive through-hole designs. Choose SMD for compact, automated assembly applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose HC-49/S for through-hole designs and cost optimization. Choose SMD packages for compact, automated assembly applications.

faqs.question: What frequency tolerance do I need for my application?
faqs.answer: Frequency tolerance requirements depend on application: ±10ppm - High-precision applications like GPS, telecom, and networking equipment. ±20ppm - Standard precision for most consumer electronics and industrial applications. ±30ppm - General purpose applications with moderate accuracy requirements. ±50ppm - Cost-sensitive applications where basic timing is sufficient. Real-time clocks typically use 32.768kHz crystals with ±20ppm tolerance. USB applications usually require 12MHz or 48MHz with ±30ppm. Ethernet applications need 25MHz with ±50ppm or better. Microcontroller clocks vary based on UART baud rate requirements - ±30ppm is typically sufficient for standard baud rates.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to calculate the exact frequency tolerance required for your specific application.

faqs.question: What are the advantages of TCXO over standard crystal oscillators?
faqs.answer: TCXO (Temperature-Compensated Crystal Oscillator) offers significant advantages over standard XO: 1) Temperature stability - TCXO maintains frequency stability across temperature variations, typically ±0.5ppm to ±2.5ppm from -40C to +85C. 2) No external compensation needed - internal circuitry compensates for temperature effects. 3) Better overall accuracy - combines crystal stability with temperature compensation. 4) Simplified system design - no need for external temperature compensation circuits. Standard XO has frequency drift of ±20ppm to ±50ppm over temperature. TCXO is essential for applications requiring stable frequency in varying temperature environments like outdoor equipment, automotive, and industrial applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use TCXO for applications requiring stable frequency across temperature variations. Use standard XO for stable temperature environments.

faqs.question: What is the typical lead time for ECEC timing components?
faqs.answer: Lead times vary by product type and frequency: Standard frequencies (8MHz, 12MHz, 16MHz, 24MHz, 48MHz) in common packages: 1-2 weeks for stock items, 4-6 weeks for non-stock. 32.768kHz tuning fork crystals: Usually stock available, 1-2 weeks delivery. Custom frequencies: 6-8 weeks depending on quantity. TCXO/VCXO/OCXO: 4-6 weeks standard, 8-10 weeks for custom specifications. Automotive-grade products: 6-8 weeks due to additional testing. BeiLuo maintains inventory of popular ECEC frequencies for fast delivery. For critical projects, we can arrange buffer stock or scheduled deliveries.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team for current lead times and availability.

categories.name: Crystal Resonators
categories.description: High-precision quartz crystal resonators in HC-49/S and SMD packages for frequency control applications from 32.768kHz to 200MHz.
categories.longDescription: ECEC crystal resonators provide stable frequency references for electronic systems. The portfolio includes HC-49/S through-hole packages and compact SMD packages from 2.0x1.6mm to 7.0x5.0mm. Frequency range spans 32.768kHz tuning fork crystals to 200MHz AT-cut crystals. These passive components require external oscillator circuits and are ideal for cost-sensitive applications. As your authorized ECEC distributor, we provide selection guidance and technical support for crystal resonator selection and integration.
categories.selectionGuide: Select frequency based on system requirements, package based on PCB space and assembly method, tolerance based on accuracy needs.

faqs.question: What is the difference between AT-cut and tuning fork crystals?
faqs.answer: AT-cut crystals are used for frequencies above 1MHz, typically 1MHz to 200MHz. They use a specific cut angle of the quartz crystal that provides excellent frequency stability over temperature. AT-cut crystals are used for microcontroller clocks, communication systems, and high-frequency applications. Tuning fork crystals operate at 32.768kHz (2^15 Hz) and are shaped like a tuning fork. They are used exclusively for real-time clock (RTC) applications due to their low frequency and low power consumption. Tuning fork crystals have higher temperature coefficients than AT-cut crystals. Choose AT-cut for MHz frequencies, tuning fork for 32.768kHz RTC applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose AT-cut crystals for MHz frequencies. Choose tuning fork crystals for 32.768kHz real-time clock applications.

faqs.question: How do I choose the right package size?
faqs.answer: Package selection depends on application requirements: HC-49/S (11.0x4.7mm) - Through-hole mounting, excellent stability, lowest cost, suitable for industrial and legacy designs. SMD 7.0x5.0mm - Large SMD for better stability and easier handling. SMD 5.0x3.2mm - Popular mid-size SMD, good balance of size and performance. SMD 3.2x2.5mm - Compact SMD for space-constrained applications. SMD 2.0x1.6mm - Ultra-compact for wearable and portable devices. Smaller packages generally have slightly worse frequency stability and higher ESR. Larger packages offer better stability and lower ESR. Consider PCB space, assembly process, and performance requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose package based on PCB space constraints, assembly method, and stability requirements. Contact us for package recommendations.

faqs.question: What is load capacitance and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Load capacitance (CL) is the effective capacitance seen by the crystal in the oscillator circuit. It is critical because: 1) The crystal is designed to oscillate at the specified frequency only when loaded with the correct capacitance. 2) Incorrect load capacitance causes frequency offset - the crystal will oscillate at a frequency different from the specified value. 3) Load capacitance affects oscillator startup and stability. The load capacitance is determined by external capacitors C1 and C2 and stray capacitance: CL = (C1 * C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray. Common CL values are 8pF, 12pF, 16pF, and 20pF. Always match the crystal's CL to your circuit design for accurate frequency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match crystal load capacitance to your oscillator circuit design. Contact us for load capacitance calculation assistance.

faqs.question: What is equivalent series resistance (ESR) and how does it affect performance?
faqs.answer: ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance) is the internal resistance of the crystal. It affects oscillator performance in several ways: 1) Startup time - lower ESR allows faster oscillator startup. 2) Power consumption - lower ESR requires less drive power. 3) Oscillator stability - higher ESR may cause startup problems or oscillation failure. Typical ESR values: HC-49/S - 20 to 100 ohms depending on frequency. SMD crystals - 30 to 150 ohms depending on package size and frequency. Smaller packages generally have higher ESR. The oscillator circuit must provide sufficient gain to overcome the ESR. Check your MCU or oscillator IC specifications for maximum allowed ESR.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose crystals with ESR within your oscillator IC specifications. Contact us for ESR requirements.

faqs.question: What is crystal aging and how does it affect long-term accuracy?
faqs.answer: Crystal aging is the gradual change in crystal frequency over time. It is caused by: 1) Mass transfer - contamination or material transfer on the crystal surface. 2) Stress relief - gradual relaxation of mounting stress. 3) Environmental effects - temperature, humidity, and mechanical stress. Typical aging rates: ±3ppm per year for standard crystals. ±1ppm per year for high-quality crystals. Aging is usually logarithmic - most change occurs in the first year. For high-precision applications, allow frequency margin for aging. Annual calibration may be required for critical applications. Store crystals in clean, stable environments to minimize aging.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider aging specifications for long-term accuracy requirements. Contact us for high-stability crystal options.

products.name: HC-49/S 8MHz Crystal Resonator
products.shortDescription: Standard HC-49/S 8MHz crystal resonator with 20pF load capacitance, ±30ppm tolerance for microcontroller applications.
products.description: The HC-49S-8MHz-20pF is a standard HC-49/S package crystal resonator designed for microcontroller clock applications.
descriptionParagraphs: This 8MHz crystal provides a stable clock source for 8-bit and 32-bit microcontrollers. The HC-49/S package offers excellent mechanical stability and is compatible with standard through-hole assembly processes.
With ±30ppm frequency tolerance and 20pF load capacitance, this crystal is suitable for most general-purpose microcontroller applications including UART communication at standard baud rates.

The industrial temperature range of -40C to +85C ensures reliable operation in harsh environments. The low-profile HC-49/S package is ideal for applications requiring through-hole mounting with height constraints.

alternativeParts.reason: Lower load capacitance for circuits with lower CL requirement
alternativeParts.useCase: Use when your oscillator circuit requires 12pF load capacitance

alternativeParts.reason: SMD package for automated assembly applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for surface mount assembly and compact PCB designs

companionParts.description: 22pF NP0 ceramic capacitor for load capacitance
ARM Cortex-M3 MCU that works with 8MHz crystal
DIP socket for easy crystal replacement

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Timing Products
faeReview.content: I highly recommend the HC-49S-8MHz-20pF for general-purpose microcontroller applications. In my experience supporting numerous embedded designs, this crystal delivers consistent performance at an excellent price point. The 20pF load capacitance is compatible with most MCU oscillator circuits, and I have found the ±30ppm tolerance adequate for standard UART baud rates up to 115200. The HC-49/S package is robust and easy to handle during assembly. For industrial applications, the -40C to +85C temperature range provides reliable operation. I always recommend using NP0/C0G ceramic capacitors for the load capacitors to ensure temperature stability. At under $0.04 in volume, this crystal offers outstanding value for cost-sensitive designs.
faeReview.highlight: Reliable 8MHz crystal with excellent cost-performance ratio for MCU applications

faqs.question: What load capacitors should I use with this crystal?
faqs.answer: For the 20pF load capacitance specification, calculate external capacitors as follows: CL = (C1 * C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray. With Cstray typically 3-5pF, you need (C1 * C2) / (C1 + C2) = 15-17pF. Using C1 = C2, each capacitor should be 30-34pF. Standard values are 33pF. Use NP0/C0G ceramic capacitors for temperature stability. Place capacitors as close to the crystal as possible to minimize stray capacitance. Always verify the actual oscillation frequency and adjust if necessary.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 33pF NP0 ceramic capacitors. Contact us for circuit design review.

faqs.question: Can this crystal be used for USB applications?
faqs.answer: For USB applications, the 8MHz frequency can be used if your MCU has a PLL that can multiply 8MHz to 48MHz for USB clock. However, USB typically requires ±2500ppm (0.25%) accuracy, and this crystal's ±30ppm tolerance is more than adequate. Check your MCU datasheet to confirm it can generate 48MHz USB clock from 8MHz input. Some MCUs require 12MHz or 48MHz crystal directly for USB. If your MCU supports PLL from 8MHz, this crystal will work well. For direct 48MHz USB clock, consider the HC-49S-48MHz-20pF instead.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify your MCU can generate USB clock from 8MHz. Contact us for USB clock recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the maximum drive level and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: The maximum drive level for this crystal is 100uW (microwatts). Drive level is the power dissipated in the crystal during oscillation. Exceeding the maximum drive level can cause: 1) Frequency shift - the crystal may oscillate at a different frequency. 2) Accelerated aging - long-term stability degradation. 3) Crystal damage - permanent damage in extreme cases. 4) Unreliable startup - difficulty starting oscillation. Most modern MCUs have low-power oscillator circuits that typically drive crystals at 10-50uW, well within the 100uW limit. If using a discrete oscillator circuit, ensure the drive level is measured and within specification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify drive level in your circuit design. Contact us for drive level measurement guidance.

faqs.question: How do I verify the crystal is oscillating at the correct frequency?
faqs.answer: To verify crystal frequency: 1) Use a frequency counter with high-impedance probe to avoid loading the crystal. 2) Measure at the oscillator output pin, not directly on the crystal pins. 3) Compare measured frequency to specified 8MHz ±30ppm (7.99976 to 8.00024 MHz). 4) Measure at operating temperature if possible. 5) Allow the circuit to warm up for stable readings. 6) Check for stable oscillation - frequency should not drift significantly. If frequency is off, check load capacitors and adjust if necessary. If crystal does not oscillate, check ESR compatibility and oscillator circuit gain.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use high-impedance frequency counter. Contact us for frequency measurement assistance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between HC-49/U and HC-49/S?
faqs.answer: HC-49/U (Universal) and HC-49/S (Short) are similar packages with slight differences: HC-49/U - Full height package (11.0 x 4.7 x 4.0mm), slightly taller profile. HC-49/S - Low profile package (11.0 x 4.7 x 3.5mm), 0.5mm shorter. Both have identical electrical characteristics and pinout. HC-49/S is more common in modern designs due to lower height profile. HC-49/U may be preferred for hand soldering due to slightly larger body. The /S variant is interchangeable with /U in most applications. This product uses the HC-49/S low-profile package.
faqs.decisionGuide: HC-49/S is recommended for most applications due to lower profile. Contact us for HC-49/U if needed.

products.name: SMD 3.2x2.5mm 16MHz Crystal
products.shortDescription: Compact SMD 3.2x2.5mm 16MHz crystal resonator with 12pF load capacitance for space-constrained applications.
products.description: The SMD3225-16MHz-12pF is a compact surface-mount crystal resonator in 3.2x2.5mm ceramic package.
descriptionParagraphs: This 16MHz crystal in a compact 3.2x2.5mm SMD package is ideal for space-constrained applications such as wearable devices, IoT sensors, and portable electronics.
The 12pF load capacitance is compatible with modern low-power MCUs. The 4-pad package provides excellent mechanical stability and reliable solder joints.
With ±20ppm frequency tolerance and industrial temperature range, this crystal delivers reliable performance in demanding environments while saving valuable PCB space.

alternativeParts.reason: Higher load capacitance for different oscillator circuits
alternativeParts.useCase: Use when your circuit requires 20pF load capacitance
Use for space-critical applications requiring smaller package

companionParts.description: Solder paste for SMD crystal assembly

faeReview.author: Sarah Johnson
faeReview.title: FAE - RF and Timing Products
faeReview.content: The SMD3225-16MHz-12pF is my go-to recommendation for compact designs requiring 16MHz clock. I have successfully used this crystal in numerous IoT and wearable projects. The 3.2x2.5mm package offers an excellent balance between size and performance - smaller than 5.0x3.2mm but more stable than 2.5x2.0mm. The 12pF load capacitance works well with modern low-power MCUs like nRF52 series. I particularly appreciate the ±20ppm tolerance which provides good accuracy for BLE and USB applications. The 4-pad package with grounded outer pads provides excellent mechanical stability. For cost-sensitive compact designs, this crystal offers great value. I recommend using 18pF load capacitors with this crystal for optimal frequency accuracy.
faeReview.highlight: Compact SMD crystal with excellent size-performance balance for IoT applications

faqs.question: What are the recommended load capacitors for this 12pF crystal?
faqs.answer: For the 12pF load capacitance: CL = (C1 * C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray. With Cstray = 3-5pF (typical for compact SMD layouts), you need effective capacitance of 7-9pF. Using C1 = C2, each capacitor should be 14-18pF. I recommend 18pF NP0/C0G ceramic capacitors in 0402 or 0603 package. The smaller 0402 package is suitable for compact designs but has slightly higher stray capacitance. Place capacitors as close as possible to the MCU crystal pins. Use ground vias near the capacitors for good grounding. Always verify actual frequency and adjust capacitors if needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 18pF NP0 capacitors. Contact us for PCB layout recommendations.

faqs.question: Is this crystal suitable for BLE applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, this 16MHz crystal is excellent for BLE applications. Nordic nRF52 series and similar BLE SoCs typically use 16MHz or 32MHz crystals. The ±20ppm tolerance meets BLE requirements which typically need ±50ppm or better. The 16MHz frequency allows the MCU PLL to generate the required 48MHz for USB (if supported) and 2.4GHz radio clock. The compact 3.2x2.5mm package is ideal for small BLE devices. The industrial temperature range supports outdoor BLE applications. I have used this crystal in multiple BLE beacon and sensor projects with reliable results. Ensure your BLE SoC supports 16MHz crystal input.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify your BLE SoC supports 16MHz crystal. Contact us for BLE crystal recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the advantage of 4-pad SMD crystals over 2-pad?
faqs.answer: 4-pad SMD crystals have two active pads (crystal connections) and two grounded pads (mechanical anchors). Advantages: 1) Better mechanical stability - grounded pads anchor the crystal to PCB, reducing vibration sensitivity. 2) Improved EMI performance - grounded pads provide shielding. 3) More reliable soldering - larger pads with better solder joint strength. 4) Consistent performance - reduced variation due to mechanical stress. The 2.0x1.6mm and smaller packages are typically 4-pad for mechanical reasons. 3.2x2.5mm and larger may be available in 2-pad or 4-pad. For high-vibration applications, 4-pad is recommended. This crystal uses 4-pad configuration for optimal reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 4-pad crystals for high-reliability and vibration-resistant applications.

faqs.question: Can this crystal be hand-soldered or is reflow required?
faqs.answer: This SMD 3.2x2.5mm crystal can be hand-soldered with care, but reflow soldering is recommended for best results. Hand soldering considerations: 1) Use fine-tip soldering iron at 300-350C. 2) Minimize heating time to prevent crystal damage. 3) Pre-heat the PCB if possible. 4) Use flux for better wetting. Reflow advantages: 1) Consistent solder joints. 2) Controlled temperature profile. 3) Better for production. 4) Reduced thermal shock. For prototyping, hand soldering is acceptable. For production, reflow is strongly recommended. The ceramic package is more sensitive to thermal shock than metal can HC-49/S. Follow ESD precautions when handling.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use reflow for production. Hand soldering acceptable for prototyping with care.

faqs.question: What is the maximum height clearance needed for this crystal?
faqs.answer: The SMD3225-16MHz-12pF has a maximum height of 0.7mm. Recommended clearance: Minimum 0.3mm above the crystal for airflow and to prevent contact with enclosures or other components. Total recommended clearance: 1.0mm above PCB surface. This low profile makes the crystal suitable for slim devices and applications with height constraints. The 0.7mm height is standard for 3.2x2.5mm ceramic package crystals. If your application has severe height constraints, consider the ultra-thin version (0.55mm max) available on request. For most applications, the standard 0.7mm height provides good reliability and manufacturability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Allow 1.0mm total height clearance. Contact us for ultra-thin options if needed.

categories.name: Crystal Oscillators
categories.description: Precision crystal oscillators including XO, TCXO, VCXO, and OCXO for stable clock generation in various applications.
categories.longDescription: ECEC crystal oscillators provide complete clock generation solutions with integrated crystal and oscillator circuitry. The portfolio includes standard XO for basic timing, TCXO for temperature-stable applications, VCXO for voltage-controlled frequency adjustment, and OCXO for ultra-high stability. These active components offer guaranteed startup, excellent frequency stability, and simplified system design. As your authorized ECEC distributor, we provide selection guidance and technical support for oscillator selection and integration.
categories.selectionGuide: Select XO for basic timing, TCXO for temperature stability, VCXO for frequency adjustment, OCXO for highest stability.

faqs.question: What is the difference between XO, TCXO, VCXO, and OCXO?
faqs.answer: XO (Crystal Oscillator) is a basic oscillator with standard frequency stability of ±20ppm to ±50ppm over temperature. TCXO (Temperature-Compensated Crystal Oscillator) includes temperature compensation circuitry achieving ±0.5ppm to ±2.5ppm stability. VCXO (Voltage-Controlled Crystal Oscillator) allows frequency adjustment via control voltage, used for PLL and synchronization. OCXO (Oven-Controlled Crystal Oscillator) uses a temperature-controlled oven to maintain constant crystal temperature, achieving ±0.01ppm to ±0.1ppm stability. Choose XO for cost-sensitive applications, TCXO for good temperature stability, VCXO for frequency control, OCXO for highest precision.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose oscillator type based on stability requirements and budget. Contact us for application-specific recommendations.

faqs.question: What supply voltage options are available?
faqs.answer: ECEC oscillators support various supply voltages: 1.8V - For low-power portable and battery-operated devices. 2.5V - For low-voltage digital systems. 3.3V - Most common voltage for modern digital systems. 5.0V - For legacy systems and industrial applications. Some oscillators support multiple voltages or have wide voltage ranges (1.8V to 3.3V). Check your system voltage and select the appropriate oscillator. 3.3V is the most popular choice for new designs. 1.8V is preferred for ultra-low-power applications. Ensure the oscillator voltage matches your system voltage for reliable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match oscillator supply voltage to your system voltage. Contact us for voltage options.

faqs.question: What output types are available?
faqs.answer: ECEC oscillators offer various output types: CMOS - Standard square wave output, most common, compatible with digital logic. LVCMOS - Low-voltage CMOS for 1.8V and 2.5V systems. LVDS - Low-Voltage Differential Signaling for high-speed, low-noise applications. LVPECL - Low-Voltage Positive Emitter-Coupled Logic for high-speed applications. HCMOS - High-speed CMOS for demanding applications. Clipped Sine - For RF and telecommunications. Choose CMOS for general applications, LVDS/LVPECL for high-speed, differential outputs for noise immunity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select output type based on your system interface requirements. Contact us for output type recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the startup time for crystal oscillators?
faqs.answer: Startup time varies by oscillator type: XO - 1ms to 10ms typical startup time. TCXO - 2ms to 50ms due to temperature compensation circuitry. VCXO - Similar to XO, 1ms to 10ms. OCXO - 1 to 5 minutes for oven to reach stable temperature. Startup time is measured from power-on to stable output frequency within specified tolerance. For fast startup requirements, choose XO or VCXO. For applications requiring immediate stable clock, consider standby power for OCXO. Startup time may vary with temperature and supply voltage. Check datasheet for specific startup time specifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider startup time requirements for your application. Contact us for fast-startup options.

faqs.question: What is phase noise and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Phase noise is the short-term random frequency fluctuations of an oscillator, measured in dBc/Hz at various offsets from the carrier frequency. It is important because: 1) Communication systems - phase noise affects signal quality and bit error rate. 2) RF applications - phase noise impacts adjacent channel interference. 3) High-speed digital - phase noise contributes to clock jitter. 4) Precision timing - phase noise affects measurement accuracy. Typical phase noise: XO - -130dBc/Hz at 1kHz offset. TCXO - -140dBc/Hz at 1kHz offset. OCXO - -150dBc/Hz or better. Lower phase noise is better. Choose oscillators with appropriate phase noise for your application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select oscillators with phase noise specifications meeting your application requirements.

products.name: 25MHz CMOS Crystal Oscillator
products.shortDescription: Standard 25MHz CMOS crystal oscillator with 3.3V supply, ±50ppm stability, and 7.0x5.0mm SMD package.
products.description: The XO-25MHz-3.3V is a standard CMOS crystal oscillator providing stable 25MHz clock output.
descriptionParagraphs: This 25MHz oscillator is ideal for Ethernet, microcontrollers, and general-purpose clock applications. The CMOS output provides clean square wave compatible with standard digital logic.
With ±50ppm frequency stability over -40C to +85C, this oscillator ensures reliable operation in industrial environments. The 3.3V supply voltage is compatible with modern digital systems.
The compact 7.0x5.0mm SMD package enables automated assembly and saves PCB space. Tristate enable/disable control allows output management for power-sensitive applications.

alternativeParts.useCase: Use for 5V systems and legacy applications

faeReview.author: David Wang
faeReview.title: FAE - Timing and Clock Products
faeReview.content: The XO-25MHz-3.3V is a reliable choice for Ethernet and general-purpose clocking applications. I have specified this oscillator in numerous industrial Ethernet and microcontroller designs with excellent results. The 25MHz frequency is the standard for 100Mbps Ethernet PHYs, and the ±50ppm stability is more than adequate for Ethernet specifications. The CMOS output provides clean edges with good drive capability for multiple loads. I particularly like the tristate enable feature which allows power management in battery-operated systems. The 7.0x5.0mm package is a good balance between size and ease of assembly. For cost-sensitive Ethernet applications, this oscillator offers excellent value.
faeReview.highlight: Reliable 25MHz oscillator for Ethernet and general clocking applications

faqs.question: Is this oscillator suitable for Gigabit Ethernet?
faqs.answer: This 25MHz oscillator is designed for 10/100Mbps Ethernet (Fast Ethernet). Gigabit Ethernet (1000Mbps) typically requires a 125MHz clock for the PHY. However, some Gigabit PHYs use 25MHz reference clock with internal PLL to generate 125MHz. Check your specific Ethernet PHY datasheet to confirm the required clock frequency. For standard 10/100Mbps Ethernet, this 25MHz oscillator is perfect. If you need 125MHz for Gigabit Ethernet, consider the XO-125MHz-3.3V. The stability and output specifications are suitable for both Fast and Gigabit Ethernet when the correct frequency is used.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify your Ethernet PHY clock requirements. Contact us for Ethernet oscillator recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the tristate function and how do I use it?
faqs.answer: The tristate (OE - Output Enable) pin allows you to enable or disable the oscillator output. When OE is high (or floating with internal pull-up), the output is active. When OE is low, the output goes to high-impedance state (tristate). This is useful for: 1) Power management - disable clock when not needed to save power. 2) Clock switching - select between multiple clock sources. 3) Testing - isolate the oscillator during board testing. 4) Hot-swapping - safely remove or insert clock without disturbing the system. Connect OE to your system control signal or leave floating for always-on operation. Check the datasheet for specific OE polarity and voltage levels.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use tristate for power management or clock switching. Contact us for application guidance.

faqs.question: What decoupling is required for the power supply?
faqs.answer: Proper power supply decoupling is essential for oscillator performance. Recommended decoupling: 1) 100nF ceramic capacitor (X7R or X5R) placed as close as possible to the VDD pin. 2) 1uF to 10uF bulk capacitor for low-frequency filtering. 3) Use short, wide traces between capacitor and oscillator. 4) Place ground vias near the capacitor ground. 5) Keep power traces away from high-speed signals. Poor decoupling can cause: Output jitter, frequency instability, increased phase noise, and startup problems. For best performance, follow the manufacturer's layout recommendations. The 100nF capacitor is the most critical for high-frequency noise filtering.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 100nF + 10uF decoupling capacitors close to VDD pin. Contact us for layout recommendations.

faqs.question: Can this oscillator drive multiple loads?
faqs.answer: The CMOS output can typically drive 10 to 15 standard CMOS loads (depending on load capacitance). For multiple loads: 1) Calculate total load capacitance (sum of all load capacitances plus trace capacitance). 2) Keep total load under 15pF for best performance. 3) Use clock buffer if driving many loads or long traces. 4) Keep trace lengths short and matched if driving multiple devices. 5) Consider using termination resistors for long traces. If the load is too heavy, you may see: Slow rise/fall times, reduced amplitude, increased jitter, and waveform distortion. For driving multiple loads, a clock buffer or multiple oscillators may be better.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate total load capacitance. Use clock buffer for heavy loads. Contact us for fan-out recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CMOS and TTL output?
faqs.answer: CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) and TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) have different output characteristics: CMOS - Rail-to-rail output swing (0V to VDD), high noise margin, low power consumption, modern standard. TTL - Output swing typically 0.4V to 2.4V (for 5V), lower noise margin, higher power consumption, legacy standard. This oscillator uses CMOS output which is compatible with most modern digital logic. CMOS provides better noise immunity and lower power. For interfacing with legacy TTL circuits, level shifting may be required. Most modern MCUs and FPGAs use CMOS logic levels and are directly compatible.
faqs.decisionGuide: CMOS is compatible with modern digital systems. Contact us for TTL output options if needed.

products.name: 26MHz TCXO with 2.0ppm Stability
products.shortDescription: High-stability 26MHz TCXO with ±2.0ppm temperature stability, ideal for GPS and cellular applications.
products.description: The TCXO-26MHz-2.0ppm is a temperature-compensated crystal oscillator providing exceptional frequency stability.
descriptionParagraphs: This 26MHz TCXO delivers ±2.0ppm stability over -40C to +85C, making it ideal for GPS, cellular, and precision timing applications where frequency accuracy is critical.

The internal temperature compensation circuitry continuously adjusts the oscillator frequency to maintain stability across temperature variations. This eliminates the need for external temperature compensation.
The compact 3.2x2.5mm package saves PCB space while providing performance comparable to larger oscillators. The 1.8V to 3.3V supply voltage range offers flexibility for various system designs.

alternativeParts.useCase: Use for high-precision GPS and timing applications
Use for cellular and communication applications

companionParts.description: Low-noise LDO for clean TCXO power

faeReview.author: Robert Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Precision Timing
faeReview.content: The TCXO-26MHz-2.0ppm is an excellent choice for GPS and cellular applications requiring high frequency stability. I have used this TCXO in multiple GPS receiver designs with outstanding results. The ±2.0ppm stability is sufficient for most GPS applications, providing fast satellite acquisition and good tracking performance. The 26MHz frequency is the standard reference for many GPS chipsets. I particularly appreciate the wide 1.8V to 3.3V voltage range which allows use in various system designs without level translation. The low phase noise of -135dBc/Hz at 1kHz offset ensures clean clock for sensitive RF applications. For GPS and precision timing applications, this TCXO offers excellent performance at a competitive price point.
faeReview.highlight: High-stability TCXO ideal for GPS and precision timing applications

faqs.question: Why do GPS applications require high-stability oscillators?
faqs.answer: GPS applications require high-stability oscillators because: 1) Frequency accuracy affects position accuracy - GPS calculates position based on time-of-flight measurements, and frequency error translates to timing error and position error. 2) Satellite acquisition - stable frequency helps the receiver quickly lock onto satellite signals. 3) Tracking performance - stable clock maintains lock during signal variations. 4) Holdover capability - during signal loss, the stable clock maintains accurate time. GPS typically requires ±0.5ppm to ±2.5ppm stability depending on application requirements. Consumer GPS can use ±2.5ppm, while survey-grade GPS may require ±0.1ppm or better. This TCXO with ±2.0ppm is suitable for most consumer and industrial GPS applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose TCXO stability based on GPS accuracy requirements. Contact us for GPS timing recommendations.

faqs.question: How does temperature compensation work in TCXO?
faqs.answer: TCXO temperature compensation works as follows: 1) Temperature sensor - measures the ambient temperature near the crystal. 2) Compensation circuit - applies a correction voltage to a varactor diode or variable capacitor in the oscillator circuit. 3) Frequency adjustment - the correction voltage slightly adjusts the oscillator frequency to counteract the crystal's natural temperature drift. 4) Continuous operation - the circuit continuously monitors temperature and adjusts compensation. The result is significantly improved frequency stability across temperature compared to standard oscillators. Typical improvement: Standard XO: ±20ppm to ±50ppm. TCXO: ±0.5ppm to ±2.5ppm. This 10x to 20x improvement makes TCXO essential for precision applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use TCXO when temperature stability is critical. Contact us for TCXO selection guidance.

faqs.question: What is the power consumption of this TCXO?
faqs.answer: The TCXO-26MHz-2.0ppm has the following power consumption: Operating current: 2.0mA typical at 3.3V, 1.5mA at 1.8V. Standby current: 10uA max (when disabled). Power consumption is higher than standard XO due to the temperature compensation circuitry. The additional power is used for: Temperature sensor operation, Compensation circuit, and Control electronics. For battery-powered applications, consider the duty cycle of operation. Use the disable function to save power when the clock is not needed. The power consumption is reasonable for the stability provided. For ultra-low-power applications, consider standard XO or power-managed operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Consider power budget for battery applications. Use disable function for power saving.

faqs.question: Can this TCXO be used for cellular applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, this 26MHz TCXO is excellent for cellular applications. Cellular systems require stable reference clocks for: RF synthesizer reference, Baseband processing clock, and Timing synchronization. The ±2.0ppm stability meets requirements for 2G/3G/4G cellular standards. The 26MHz frequency is commonly used for cellular chipsets. The low phase noise ensures clean RF transmission and reception. I have successfully used this TCXO in GSM, LTE, and NB-IoT modem designs. The wide voltage range (1.8V to 3.3V) accommodates various cellular chipset requirements. For cellular IoT applications, this TCXO provides reliable performance at a competitive cost.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify your cellular chipset reference clock requirements. Contact us for cellular timing recommendations.

faqs.question: What layout considerations are important for TCXO?
faqs.answer: TCXO layout considerations: 1) Power supply - use clean, well-decoupled power (100nF + 1uF capacitors close to VDD). 2) Grounding - solid ground plane under the TCXO, minimize ground impedance. 3) Thermal management - keep TCXO away from heat sources, ensure good airflow. 4) Output routing - short traces to load, avoid crossing high-speed signals. 5) Shielding - consider ground ring or shielding for sensitive applications. 6) No vias under TCXO - avoid vias in the crystal area to prevent mechanical stress. 7) Keep-out zone - maintain clearance from high-frequency or high-current traces. Good layout ensures the TCXO achieves its specified stability and phase noise performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow layout guidelines for optimal TCXO performance. Contact us for layout review.

categories.name: MEMS Oscillators
categories.description: Compact MEMS-based oscillators offering high reliability, programmable frequencies, and excellent shock resistance for modern electronics.
categories.longDescription: ECEC MEMS oscillators use Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems technology to provide highly reliable timing solutions. Unlike quartz crystals, MEMS oscillators are immune to shock and vibration, offer programmable frequencies, and have faster startup times. They are ideal for automotive, industrial, and portable applications where reliability is critical. As your authorized ECEC distributor, we provide selection guidance and technical support for MEMS oscillator selection and integration.
categories.selectionGuide: Select MEMS oscillators for high reliability, shock resistance, and programmable frequency requirements.

faqs.question: What are the advantages of MEMS oscillators over quartz?
faqs.answer: MEMS oscillators offer several advantages over quartz: 1) Shock resistance - MEMS devices can withstand 50,000g shock vs 5,000g for quartz. 2) Vibration immunity - MEMS performance is unaffected by vibration. 3) Programmable frequency - single device can be programmed to any frequency. 4) Faster startup - MEMS startup in milliseconds vs seconds for OCXO. 5) Smaller size - MEMS can be packaged in CSP or tiny QFN. 6) No activity dips - MEMS don't have the frequency jumps seen in quartz. 7) Better aging - MEMS typically have lower long-term drift. Choose MEMS for high-reliability, high-shock, or programmable frequency applications. Quartz remains cost-effective for standard applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose MEMS for high-reliability and harsh environments. Choose quartz for cost-sensitive standard applications.

faqs.question: What is the frequency stability of MEMS oscillators?
faqs.answer: MEMS oscillator frequency stability varies by grade: Standard MEMS XO - ±20ppm to ±50ppm over -40C to +85C, comparable to quartz XO. MEMS TCXO - ±0.5ppm to ±2.5ppm with temperature compensation, comparable to quartz TCXO. High-grade MEMS - ±0.1ppm to ±0.5ppm for precision applications. Stability factors: Temperature range - wider ranges have more variation. Supply voltage - stability specified over voltage range. Aging - typically ±1ppm to ±3ppm per year. Load conditions - stability over load variations. MEMS stability has improved significantly and now matches or exceeds quartz for most applications. Check specific datasheet for exact stability specifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select MEMS grade based on stability requirements. Contact us for precision MEMS options.

faqs.question: Can MEMS oscillators be programmed to any frequency?
faqs.answer: Yes, programmable MEMS oscillators can generate a wide range of frequencies: Frequency range - typically 1MHz to 200MHz or higher. Resolution - programmable in 1ppm steps or better. Programming method - factory programmed or field programmable via I2C/SPI. Single device - one part number can be programmed to any frequency in range. Benefits: Reduced inventory - stock one device for multiple frequencies. Design flexibility - change frequency without hardware change. Faster prototyping - program to desired frequency immediately. Non-programmable MEMS are also available for fixed-frequency applications at lower cost. Programmable MEMS are ideal for applications requiring multiple frequencies or design flexibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose programmable MEMS for design flexibility. Choose fixed-frequency MEMS for cost optimization.

faqs.question: Are MEMS oscillators suitable for automotive applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, MEMS oscillators are excellent for automotive applications due to: 1) High shock resistance - withstands automotive shock and vibration. 2) Wide temperature range - qualified for -40C to +125C or +150C. 3) AEC-Q100 qualified - meets automotive reliability standards. 4) No frequency jumps - stable operation under stress. 5) Long-term reliability - proven in automotive environments. Automotive applications: Engine control units, Transmission control, ADAS systems, Infotainment, Body electronics. MEMS reliability in harsh automotive environments often exceeds quartz performance. The higher cost of MEMS is justified by the improved reliability and reduced field failures.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose AEC-Q100 qualified MEMS for automotive applications. Contact us for automotive MEMS recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the power consumption of MEMS oscillators?
faqs.answer: MEMS oscillator power consumption varies by type and frequency: Low-frequency MEMS (32.768kHz) - 1uA to 5uA, ideal for RTC applications. Standard MEMS XO (1MHz to 50MHz) - 1mA to 5mA at 3.3V. High-frequency MEMS (>50MHz) - 5mA to 15mA. MEMS TCXO - 2mA to 5mA including compensation circuitry. Power-saving features: Standby mode - <1uA when disabled. Frequency selection - lower frequencies consume less power. Voltage optimization - operate at lowest acceptable voltage. MEMS power consumption is generally comparable to or better than equivalent quartz oscillators. The excellent startup time also allows aggressive power management with frequent on/off cycles.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select MEMS based on power budget. Use standby mode for battery applications.

products.name: 50MHz MEMS Oscillator
products.shortDescription: High-reliability 50MHz MEMS oscillator with ±25ppm stability, 2.5x2.0mm package, and excellent shock resistance.
products.description: The MEMS-50MHz-25ppm is a high-reliability MEMS-based oscillator providing stable 50MHz output.
descriptionParagraphs: This MEMS oscillator uses advanced micro-electromechanical systems technology to provide exceptional reliability and shock resistance. The device can withstand 50,000g shock and operates reliably in high-vibration environments.

With ±25ppm stability over -40C to +85C, this oscillator is suitable for industrial and automotive applications. The 50MHz frequency is ideal for high-speed microcontrollers, networking equipment, and communication systems.
The ultra-compact 2.5x2.0mm package saves PCB space while providing performance superior to larger quartz oscillators. The 1.8V to 3.3V supply voltage range offers design flexibility.

alternativeParts.reason: 48MHz for USB applications requiring exact frequency
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for USB 2.0 applications requiring 48MHz clock
Use for cost-sensitive applications with relaxed stability requirements

faeReview.author: James Zhang
faeReview.title: FAE - MEMS and Advanced Timing
faeReview.content: The MEMS-50MHz-25ppm is my top recommendation for high-reliability applications. I have used this MEMS oscillator in automotive and aerospace projects where shock and vibration are major concerns. The 50,000g shock rating is genuinely impressive - far exceeding what any quartz oscillator can survive. The 50MHz frequency is perfect for high-speed ARM Cortex-M4/M7 microcontrollers. I particularly appreciate the fast 3ms startup time which enables aggressive power management. The ±25ppm stability is adequate for most industrial applications. While the cost is higher than quartz, the reliability improvement is worth it for critical applications. For automotive ECUs and industrial control systems, this MEMS oscillator provides peace of mind.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-reliable MEMS oscillator with exceptional shock resistance for demanding applications

faqs.question: How does MEMS technology provide shock resistance?
faqs.answer: MEMS oscillators achieve shock resistance through: 1) Microscopic size - the resonating element is extremely small (micrometers), making it inherently robust. 2) Silicon construction - single-crystal silicon is mechanically strong and elastic. 3) Encapsulation - the MEMS structure is hermetically sealed in a protective cavity. 4) Center-anchored design - the resonator is anchored at its center, minimizing stress from external forces. 5) High resonant frequency - MEMS resonate at MHz frequencies, far above typical shock frequencies. In contrast, quartz crystals are larger, more brittle, and can fracture under shock. The MEMS design eliminates the failure modes that affect quartz, providing 10x better shock resistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose MEMS for applications with shock, vibration, or mechanical stress.

faqs.question: What applications benefit most from MEMS oscillators?
faqs.answer: Applications that benefit most from MEMS oscillators: 1) Automotive - engine control, transmission, chassis systems with high vibration. 2) Aerospace - avionics, satellites, drones with extreme shock and radiation. 3) Industrial - factory automation, robotics, heavy machinery. 4) Portable devices - smartphones, tablets subject to drops and impacts. 5) Military - ruggedized equipment for harsh environments. 6) Medical - implantable devices requiring extreme reliability. Key benefits in these applications: Shock survival, Vibration immunity, Long-term stability, Small size, Low power. The higher cost of MEMS is justified by improved reliability and reduced field failures in critical applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use MEMS for high-reliability and harsh environment applications. Contact us for application guidance.

faqs.question: Can MEMS oscillators replace quartz in all applications?
faqs.answer: MEMS oscillators can replace quartz in most applications, but considerations apply: Advantages of MEMS: Better shock/vibration resistance, Programmable frequencies, Faster startup, Smaller size. Limitations of MEMS: Higher cost (2x to 5x quartz), Slightly higher power, Phase noise may be higher than premium quartz. Applications where quartz remains preferred: Ultra-low phase noise RF, Lowest cost consumer products, Legacy designs without shock concerns. For new designs requiring reliability, MEMS is often the better choice. For cost-sensitive consumer products, quartz remains competitive. The decision depends on specific application requirements and cost constraints.
faqs.decisionGuide: Evaluate MEMS vs quartz based on reliability requirements and cost budget.

faqs.question: What is the long-term reliability of MEMS oscillators?
faqs.answer: MEMS oscillators have excellent long-term reliability: FIT rate - typically <10 FIT (Failures In Time per billion hours). MTBF - Mean Time Between Failures >100 million hours. Aging - ±1ppm to ±3ppm per year, better than many quartz oscillators. Field data - billions of MEMS oscillators deployed with low failure rates. Reliability advantages: No moving parts in traditional sense, Hermetic sealing prevents contamination, Silicon material is stable over time, No activity dips or frequency jumps. Qualification testing: High-temperature operating life, Temperature cycling, Mechanical shock and vibration, High-acceleration testing. MEMS reliability meets or exceeds AEC-Q100 automotive standards.
faqs.decisionGuide: MEMS reliability is excellent for critical applications. Contact us for reliability data.

faqs.question: How do I handle soldering and assembly of MEMS oscillators?
faqs.answer: MEMS oscillator soldering guidelines: Temperature profile - standard lead-free reflow profile is acceptable. Peak temperature - 260C max, typical 245-250C. Preheat - follow standard ramp rates to prevent thermal shock. Hand soldering - possible with care, limit iron temperature to 350C and time to 3 seconds. PCB layout - follow manufacturer recommendations for grounding and decoupling. Cleaning - avoid ultrasonic cleaning which can damage MEMS structures. ESD protection - handle with standard ESD precautions. MEMS devices are generally more robust than quartz during assembly due to their silicon construction and packaging. Standard SMT assembly processes work well for MEMS oscillators.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard SMT assembly processes. Avoid ultrasonic cleaning.

products.name: 32.768kHz MEMS RTC Oscillator
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low power 32.768kHz MEMS oscillator for real-time clock applications with 100ppm stability.
products.description: The MEMS-32.768kHz-100ppm is an ultra-low power MEMS oscillator designed for RTC applications.
descriptionParagraphs: This 32.768kHz MEMS oscillator provides the standard frequency for real-time clock applications with extremely low power consumption of only 1.5µA.
The MEMS technology eliminates the need for external load capacitors, simplifying circuit design and reducing BOM count. The device starts up in just 5ms.
With excellent shock resistance of 50,000g and wide temperature range, this oscillator is ideal for portable and battery-powered devices including wearables, IoT sensors, and medical devices.

alternativeParts.useCase: Use for applications requiring better timekeeping accuracy
Use for lowest cost RTC applications

companionParts.description: Coin cell battery for RTC backup

faeReview.author: Lisa Wang
faeReview.title: FAE - Low Power Timing
faeReview.content: The MEMS-32.768kHz-100ppm is my go-to recommendation for RTC applications requiring ultra-low power. I have used this oscillator in numerous wearable and IoT designs with excellent results. The 1.5µA current consumption is significantly lower than quartz alternatives which typically require 5-10µA plus load capacitor leakage. The elimination of external load capacitors simplifies the design and reduces BOM cost. The 32.768kHz frequency is the standard for RTCs and the 100ppm accuracy is sufficient for most timekeeping applications (about 8.6 seconds per day drift). For battery-powered devices where every microamp counts, this MEMS oscillator is an excellent choice.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low power MEMS RTC oscillator ideal for battery-powered devices

faqs.question: Why choose MEMS over quartz for 32.768kHz RTC?
faqs.answer: MEMS offers advantages for RTC applications: 1) Lower power - 1.5µA vs 5-10µA for quartz plus capacitor leakage. 2) No load capacitors - simplifies design, reduces BOM. 3) Better shock resistance - 50,000g vs 5,000g for quartz. 4) Faster startup - 5ms vs 100-500ms for quartz. 5) Smaller size - 2.0x1.6mm vs larger quartz packages. 6) No activity dips - smooth frequency vs temperature. Trade-offs: Slightly higher cost than basic quartz, 100ppm accuracy vs 20ppm for high-end quartz. For battery-powered and portable applications, MEMS advantages typically outweigh the cost difference.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose MEMS for battery-powered applications. Choose quartz for lowest cost if power is not critical.

faqs.question: How accurate is 100ppm for timekeeping?
faqs.answer: 100ppm (parts per million) accuracy means: Daily drift - 100ppm × 86,400 seconds = 8.64 seconds per day. Monthly drift - about 4.3 minutes per month. Yearly drift - about 52 minutes per year. This accuracy is sufficient for: General timekeeping, Consumer electronics, IoT devices, Most non-critical applications. For better accuracy, consider: 50ppm MEMS oscillator - 4.3 seconds/day drift. TCXO - <1 second/day drift. GPS synchronization - eliminates drift entirely. For most applications, 100ppm is adequate and the ultra-low power consumption is the primary benefit.
faqs.decisionGuide: 100ppm is sufficient for most RTC applications. Contact us for higher accuracy options if needed.

faqs.question: Can this oscillator be used with any RTC chip?
faqs.answer: This MEMS oscillator is compatible with most RTC chips: Output type - CMOS square wave compatible with digital inputs. Frequency - standard 32.768kHz used by all RTCs. Voltage - 1.5V to 3.3V matches common RTC supplies. No load caps - RTC sees clean clock signal. Connection - connect output to RTC X1/CLKIN pin, leave X2 unconnected. Check your RTC datasheet for: Input voltage levels, Clock input pin name, Any specific requirements. Most modern RTCs accept external clock input. Some older RTCs may require specific interface - check compatibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: Compatible with most RTC chips. Verify your RTC accepts external clock input.

faqs.question: What is the battery life impact?
faqs.answer: Battery life impact of this MEMS oscillator: Current consumption - 1.5µA typical. Battery capacity - typical CR2032 coin cell = 225mAh. Backup time calculation - 225mAh / 1.5µA = 150,000 hours = 17 years. Compare to quartz: Quartz oscillator - 5-10µA. Load capacitor leakage - 1-5µA. Total quartz - 6-15µA. Battery life with quartz - 225mAh / 10µA = 22,500 hours = 2.6 years. The MEMS oscillator extends battery life by 6-8x compared to typical quartz solutions. This is critical for devices requiring long battery life without replacement.
faqs.decisionGuide: MEMS significantly extends battery life. Ideal for long-life battery-powered devices.

faqs.question: Does the oscillator need decoupling capacitors?
faqs.answer: Yes, power supply decoupling is recommended: Required capacitor - 100nF ceramic close to VDD pin. Optional - 1µF bulk capacitor for additional filtering. Layout - place capacitor within 2mm of VDD pin. Benefits - ensures stable operation, reduces jitter, prevents noise coupling. The oscillator is low power but still benefits from clean power. The decoupling is simpler than quartz which requires load capacitors. Total capacitors needed: MEMS - 1 decoupling cap. Quartz - 2 load caps + 1 decoupling cap. This simplification is one of the advantages of MEMS oscillators.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 100nF decoupling capacitor. Simpler than quartz load capacitor requirements.

categories.name: Timing Modules
categories.description: Integrated timing modules and clock solutions providing multiple outputs and advanced features for complex system requirements.
categories.longDescription: ECEC timing modules provide complete clock generation and distribution solutions in integrated packages. These modules include multiple clock outputs, PLL circuits, frequency synthesis, and advanced features like spread spectrum and clock redundancy. They simplify system design by providing all necessary timing from a single device. As your authorized ECEC distributor, we provide selection guidance and technical support for timing module selection and integration.
categories.selectionGuide: Select timing modules based on number of outputs, frequency requirements, and special features needed.

faqs.question: What are the advantages of using a timing module vs discrete oscillators?
faqs.answer: Timing modules offer advantages over discrete oscillators: 1) Single reference - one crystal or reference input generates all clocks. 2) Synchronized outputs - all clocks are phase-locked and synchronized. 3) Frequency synthesis - generate any frequency from single reference. 4) Multiple outputs - 2 to 12+ outputs from one device. 5) Reduced BOM - fewer components, lower inventory. 6) Better jitter performance - integrated PLL optimization. 7) Advanced features - spread spectrum, redundancy, fail-safe. 8) Simplified design - complete solution in one device. Disadvantages: Higher cost than single oscillator, Single point of failure. For systems requiring multiple related clocks, timing modules simplify design and improve performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use timing modules for multi-clock systems. Use discrete oscillators for single-clock applications.

faqs.question: What is spread spectrum clocking and when should I use it?
faqs.answer: Spread spectrum clocking (SSC) modulates the clock frequency to reduce EMI (Electromagnetic Interference). How it works: The clock frequency is varied slightly (typically ±0.5% to ±2%) at a low rate (30kHz to 60kHz). This spreads the energy over a wider frequency range, reducing peak emissions. Benefits: Reduced EMI by 6dB to 20dB, Easier EMC compliance, Reduced shielding requirements. When to use: High-speed digital systems, Consumer electronics with EMC requirements, Systems with sensitive analog circuits, Cost-sensitive designs where shielding is expensive. Spread spectrum is commonly used in: PCs and laptops, Displays and TVs, Networking equipment, Automotive electronics. Most timing modules offer optional spread spectrum.
faqs.decisionGuide: Enable spread spectrum for EMI-sensitive applications. Contact us for SSC recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I select the right number of outputs?
faqs.answer: Selecting the number of outputs: Count your clock requirements - list all devices needing clocks. Group by frequency - identify which devices can share the same frequency. Consider redundancy - add extra outputs for fail-over if needed. Future expansion - add 20-30% extra for design margin. Common configurations: 2-4 outputs - small systems, single board. 6-8 outputs - medium systems, multiple boards. 10-12+ outputs - large systems, servers, telecom. Output types: LVCMOS for general digital, LVDS/LVPECL for high-speed, Differential for noise immunity. Mixed output types available on some modules. Choose a module with slightly more outputs than currently needed for future flexibility.
faqs.decisionGuide: Count clock requirements and add margin. Contact us for multi-output recommendations.

faqs.question: What is clock jitter and how does it affect my system?
faqs.answer: Clock jitter is the variation in clock edge timing from the ideal position. Types of jitter: Period jitter - variation in clock period. Cycle-to-cycle jitter - variation between adjacent cycles. Phase jitter (RMS) - integrated phase noise. Long-term jitter - variation over many cycles. Effects on systems: Digital systems - reduced timing margin, potential errors. ADC/DAC - reduced SNR, lower effective bits. Communication systems - increased bit error rate. High-speed interfaces - reduced eye opening. Typical jitter specs: Standard oscillators - 1ps to 10ps RMS. Low-jitter oscillators - 0.1ps to 1ps RMS. Timing modules with PLL - 0.5ps to 3ps RMS depending on design. For high-speed SERDES, use low-jitter timing solutions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select low-jitter timing for high-speed interfaces. Contact us for jitter requirements.

faqs.question: Can timing modules accept different input references?
faqs.answer: Yes, timing modules support various input references: Crystal input - 10MHz to 50MHz fundamental crystal. Clock input - CMOS, LVDS, or LVPECL clock signal. Frequency range - modules accept wide input frequency range. Automatic detection - some modules auto-detect input type. Redundant inputs - switch between primary and backup references. Input requirements: Frequency accuracy - typically ±100ppm input tolerance. Amplitude - CMOS (0-VDD), differential (0.2V to 1.2V). Duty cycle - 40% to 60% typical. Input impedance - 50 ohms or high-Z options. Benefits: Design flexibility - use crystal or external clock. Redundancy - backup reference for reliability. System integration - accept clock from another system. Check datasheet for specific input requirements and capabilities.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify input compatibility with your reference source. Contact us for input options.

products.name: 6-Output PCIe Clock Generator
products.shortDescription: Integrated 6-output clock generator for PCIe applications with spread spectrum and low jitter.
products.description: The CG-6OUT-PCIe is a dedicated clock generator providing six PCIe-compliant clock outputs.
descriptionParagraphs: This clock generator provides six 100MHz differential outputs meeting PCIe Gen1/Gen2/Gen3 requirements. The integrated PLL provides excellent jitter performance for high-speed serial links.
Spread spectrum capability reduces EMI by 8dB to 12dB, simplifying system EMC compliance. The 25MHz crystal input accepts standard crystals.
The 5.0x5.0mm QFN package integrates all necessary circuitry, reducing BOM count and PCB area compared to discrete solutions.

alternativeParts.useCase: Use for systems requiring fewer PCIe clocks

alternativeParts.reason: PCIe Gen4 compatible with lower jitter
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for PCIe Gen4 applications requiring <0.3ps jitter

faeReview.author: Kevin Wu
faeReview.title: FAE - High-Speed Clocking
faeReview.content: The CG-6OUT-PCIe is an excellent choice for PCIe clock distribution. I have used this clock generator in multiple server and workstation designs with great success. The 0.5ps jitter performance easily meets PCIe Gen3 requirements, and I have verified compliance in testing. The spread spectrum feature is valuable for passing EMI testing - I typically see 8-10dB reduction in clock harmonics. The HCSL outputs are directly compatible with PCIe slots and devices. I particularly appreciate the integrated termination resistors which save board space. For systems requiring multiple PCIe clocks, this single device replaces six discrete oscillators, reducing cost and complexity. The 25MHz crystal input accepts standard crystals readily available from our inventory.
faeReview.highlight: Integrated PCIe clock solution with excellent jitter and EMI performance

faqs.question: What PCIe generations does this clock support?
faqs.answer: This clock generator supports PCIe Gen1, Gen2, and Gen3: PCIe Gen1 (2.5GT/s) - requires <75ps RMS jitter, this device provides <0.5ps. PCIe Gen2 (5.0GT/s) - requires <3.1ps RMS jitter, this device easily meets. PCIe Gen3 (8.0GT/s) - requires <1.0ps RMS jitter, this device provides <0.5ps. The 100MHz frequency and HCSL output levels meet PCIe specifications. The spread spectrum is PCIe-compliant with 0.5% down-spread. For PCIe Gen4 (16.0GT/s), check specific jitter requirements as they are more stringent. This clock generator is ideal for motherboards, add-in cards, and embedded systems using PCIe.
faqs.decisionGuide: Compatible with PCIe Gen1/Gen2/Gen3. Contact us for Gen4 requirements.

faqs.question: How do I enable spread spectrum and what are the effects?
faqs.answer: Spread spectrum is enabled via the SS_EN pin: Connect SS_EN to VDD - spread spectrum enabled (default). Connect SS_EN to GND - spread spectrum disabled. Effects of spread spectrum: EMI reduction - 8dB to 12dB reduction in clock harmonics. Frequency modulation - clock varies ±0.5% around center frequency at 30kHz rate. System effects - minimal impact on PCIe performance, slight increase in jitter (still within spec). When to enable: Always enable for production systems to ensure EMC compliance. Disable only for testing or debugging. The down-spread modulation centers the average frequency at the nominal value. PCIe devices are designed to tolerate spread spectrum clocks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Enable spread spectrum for production to ensure EMC compliance.

faqs.question: What is HCSL output and how do I interface with it?
faqs.answer: HCSL (High-Speed Current Steering Logic) is the standard output for PCIe clocks: Differential output - provides positive (CLK+) and negative (CLK-) signals. Low voltage swing - 0.7V to 1.2V differential swing. Current mode - 14mA current source, requires termination. Termination - 50 ohms to ground at receiver end. Benefits: Low EMI, good signal integrity, PCIe compatible. Interfacing: Connect CLK+ to PCIe device clock input positive. Connect CLK- to PCIe device clock input negative. Place 50 ohm termination resistors near the receiver. Keep trace lengths matched and short (<6 inches). HCSL is the industry standard for PCIe reference clocks and is supported by all PCIe devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard HCSL termination at receiver. Contact us for layout recommendations.

faqs.question: Can I use this clock generator for non-PCIe applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, this clock generator can be used for non-PCIe applications: General 100MHz clocking - HCSL can be converted to single-ended if needed. High-speed serial - suitable for SATA, USB3, Ethernet with proper termination. FPGA clocking - many FPGAs accept differential clocks. Processor clocking - check specific processor requirements. Considerations: Output is 100MHz fixed frequency. HCSL output level may need translation for CMOS inputs. Spread spectrum may not be desired for some applications. For non-PCIe use, verify: Input frequency requirements, Output level compatibility, Jitter requirements. The excellent jitter performance makes this suitable for many high-speed applications beyond PCIe.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify compatibility with your specific application requirements.

faqs.question: What power supply decoupling is required?
faqs.answer: Power supply decoupling for this clock generator: 3.3V VDD pins - 0.1uF ceramic capacitor per pin, placed close to pin. Bulk decoupling - 1uF to 10uF near the device. Ground connections - solid ground plane, multiple vias to ground. Layout recommendations: Keep decoupling capacitors within 2mm of power pins. Use short, wide traces for power distribution. Avoid vias in high-frequency current paths. Separate analog and digital grounds if applicable. Filter noise - consider ferrite bead for noisy systems. The clock generator is sensitive to power supply noise. Good decoupling ensures low jitter and stable operation. Follow the evaluation board layout for best performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 0.1uF per power pin plus bulk capacitance. Contact us for layout review.

products.name: 8-Output LVDS Clock Buffer
products.shortDescription: High-performance 8-output LVDS clock buffer with additive jitter of 0.1ps for high-speed clock distribution.
products.description: The CB-8OUT-LVDS is a high-performance clock buffer designed for distributing low-jitter clocks to multiple destinations.
descriptionParagraphs: This clock buffer accepts a differential LVDS or LVPECL input and provides eight identical LVDS outputs with ultra-low additive jitter of only 0.1ps RMS.
The device is ideal for high-speed ADC/DAC clocking, FPGA clock distribution, and telecommunications equipment where maintaining signal integrity is critical.
With support for frequencies up to 1.5GHz and wide operating temperature range, this buffer meets the demanding requirements of high-performance systems.

alternativeParts.reason: 4-output version for smaller clock distribution needs
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for systems requiring fewer clock outputs

alternativeParts.reason: LVPECL output version for high-speed applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for applications requiring LVPECL output levels

companionParts.description: Low-noise LDO for clock buffer power

faeReview.author: Alex Chen
faeReview.title: FAE - High-Speed Clock Distribution
faeReview.content: The CB-8OUT-LVDS is an excellent clock buffer for high-performance applications. I have used this buffer in multiple high-speed ADC and FPGA designs where low jitter is critical. The 0.1ps additive jitter is among the best in the industry and has minimal impact on the source clock quality. The LVDS outputs provide excellent signal integrity even over long PCB traces. I particularly appreciate the flexibility of 2.5V or 3.3V operation, allowing use in various system designs. The 8 outputs can drive multiple ADCs or FPGA banks from a single clock source, simplifying system architecture. For high-speed data converter applications, this buffer ensures sampling clock quality is maintained throughout the system.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low jitter clock buffer ideal for high-speed ADC and FPGA applications

faqs.question: What is additive jitter and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Additive jitter is the additional jitter introduced by the clock buffer: Definition - jitter added to the input clock signal as it passes through the buffer. Measurement - compare output jitter to input jitter, the difference is additive jitter. Importance - cumulative jitter degrades system timing margin. This buffer - 0.1ps additive jitter is extremely low. Impact - minimal degradation of clock quality. Typical values - standard buffers add 0.3-1.0ps, this buffer adds only 0.1ps. For high-speed systems: ADC/DAC sampling clocks - low additive jitter maintains SNR. High-speed serial - low jitter ensures bit error rate specs. FPGA clocks - low jitter preserves timing margin. This buffer's 0.1ps performance is suitable for the most demanding applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose this buffer for applications where preserving clock quality is critical.

faqs.question: How do I terminate LVDS outputs properly?
faqs.answer: LVDS output termination requirements: Termination resistor - 100 ohms differential across the output pair. Placement - place resistor at the receiver end of the trace. Trace routing - route as 100 ohm differential pair. Trace length - keep traces as short as possible, <6 inches recommended. Impedance control - maintain 100 ohm differential impedance. Stub length - minimize stubs at the receiver. This buffer has integrated current sources, so only the 100 ohm termination resistor is needed at the receiver. No additional resistors at the buffer output. For multiple loads: Point-to-point - best signal integrity, one receiver per output. Daisy chain - possible for closely spaced receivers. Star routing - avoid, causes reflections. Follow these guidelines for best signal integrity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 100 ohm differential termination at receiver. Contact us for layout recommendations.

faqs.question: Can I use this buffer with single-ended clock inputs?
faqs.answer: This buffer requires differential input: Input type - LVDS or LVPECL differential only. Single-ended - not directly supported. Conversion options: Single-ended to differential converter IC. Transformer coupling - for high frequencies. Resistive network - converts CMOS to LVDS (not recommended for precision). Recommended approach: Use a differential oscillator as the clock source. Most precision oscillators offer LVDS or LVPECL outputs. This ensures best signal integrity from source to destination. If you must use single-ended: Consider CB-CMOS-8OUT for CMOS inputs. Add external single-ended to differential converter. Contact us for application guidance. For best performance, use differential signaling throughout the clock path.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use differential clock source for best performance. Contact us for single-ended alternatives.

faqs.question: What is the maximum fan-out for each output?
faqs.answer: Fan-out recommendations for LVDS outputs: Standard load - one LVDS receiver per output (point-to-point). Multiple loads - daisy chain up to 2-3 closely spaced receivers. Maximum - depends on trace length and receiver input capacitance. Loading effects: Each receiver adds ~3pF input capacitance. Multiple receivers degrade signal edge rates. Reflections occur with improper termination. Best practices: Point-to-point - one receiver per output for best signal integrity. Use multiple outputs - buffer has 8 outputs for distribution. Clock tree - use multiple buffers for large fan-out. Simulation - simulate clock distribution for critical designs. This buffer is designed to drive standard LVDS loads. For driving many loads, consider using multiple buffers in a clock tree architecture.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use point-to-point for best signal integrity. Contact us for large fan-out solutions.

faqs.question: How do I power the buffer for lowest jitter?
faqs.answer: Power supply recommendations for lowest jitter: Voltage selection - 2.5V or 3.3V, choose based on system. Lower voltage - 2.5V may have slightly lower noise. Decoupling - 0.1uF ceramic per power pin, close to device. Bulk capacitance - 1-10uF near the device. Power supply noise - use clean, low-noise supply. LDO regulator - recommended for clock buffer power. Avoid: Switching noise - keep away from switching power supplies. Shared supplies - avoid sharing with noisy digital circuits. Long traces - keep power traces short and wide. Layout tips: Solid ground plane under buffer. Multiple vias for ground connections. Keep decoupling caps within 2mm. Follow evaluation board layout. Good power supply design is essential for achieving the specified 0.1ps additive jitter performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use clean LDO power with adequate decoupling. Contact us for power design recommendations.
